summary.title: Summary
summary.body:
## Summary

This file is the compact workflow classifier for programming projects.

Read it after `STATE.json` when the user gives a code-project command or asks for implementation work.

read_order.title: Read Order
read_order.body:
## Read Order

For ordinary code work:

1. `AGENTS.md`
2. `STATE.json`
3. `PIPELINE-CONFIG.json`
4. `WORKFLOW.json` only when priority steering, feature triage, planning, or broad project-risk judgment matters
5. `team/*.json` only when the user names a role, asks for team-style review, or WORKFLOW.json routes the task to that role
6. `core/PROJECT-PROFILE.json` when `project_profile` exists
7. `core/ROI-BIAS.json`
8. `core/TASK-WORK.json` for broad multi-step work
9. `_PLAN.json`
10. smallest relevant file under `context-docs/`
11. source files in the touched ownership slice

Context tier rule: Tier 1 is the small always-loaded core and decision gates; Tier 2 is routed operating rules such as this workflow and task/profile guidance; Tier 3 is deep patterns, archetypes, and project-specific references; Tier 4 is history, vision, and simplified backlog; Tier 5 is FOMO or inactive inspiration. Read `core/CONTEXT-TIERS.json` when changing startup context, directive hierarchy, context budget, knowledge tier metadata, or knowledgebase retagging.

For every programming implementation, code modification, refactor, bugfix, code review, or any task that may touch source code, load the full compiled code-guidance set before the first code read/write that could lead to an edit:

1. Prefer `tools/knowledge/knowledge_code_context.py` when available. It must load the full base guidance slice and write the local `ignored/code_context_gate.json` marker after success.
2. Pass the most relevant named view when it exists, such as `--views architecture`, `--views performance`, `--views debugging`, or another project-specific architecture/performance view. Treat views as additive task context, not replacements for the full base set.
3. If the wrapper is unavailable, run `tools/knowledge/knowledge_check.ps1`, then load all currently compiled engineering, coding, architecture, best-practices, borrowed-before-invented, production-grade software engineering, DDD-ADIV, future-first, data-first ownership, runtime, diagnostics, performance, and verification rules.
4. If SQLite is unavailable, use `tools/knowledge/knowledge_file_select.ps1` with the same tag/type intent.
5. `tools/repo/check_task.ps1` should fail changed-code checks when the fresh full-guidance marker is missing, stale, or does not include the required base tags.

For every user command or substantive question, perform a context-tag pass before answering or executing:

1. Load the main workflow and instruction documents required by the active adapter/project profile.
2. Run `tools/knowledge/context_tags.py <user text>` when available to derive relevant compiled-knowledge tags from the user's command or question.
3. Query notes from the compiled knowledge layer with `tools/knowledge/knowledge_select.py --tags <tags> --match any`; if SQLite is unavailable, use `tools/knowledge/knowledge_file_select.ps1` with the same tag intent.
4. Use those notes to choose the smallest correct workflow, reads, risks, and implementation boundary before answering or editing.
5. Do not let tag-selected notes replace exact source reads when changing source files, making high-stakes claims, or resolving ambiguity.

For broad architecture, ownership, persistence, API, build, toolchain, or registry work, also read:

1. `core/PROMOTION-RULES.json`
2. `project/code/DDD-ADIV.json`
3. `project/code/GIT.json`
4. `project/code/FROZEN-LAYERS.json`

For branch-scoped projects, also read:

1. `project/code/BRANCH-MODE.json`
2. branch state and plan from the configured `workflow.branch_files_dir`

For raw user instruction buffers, also read:

1. `core/INSTRUCTION-CAPTURE.json`
2. `project/code/INSTRUCTION-CAPTURE.json`

For logs, traces, crashes, console output, and evidence-driven debugging, also read:

1. `project/code/DIAGNOSTICS.json`

command_classifier.title: Command Classifier
command_classifier.body:
## Command Classifier

`CONTINUE`: execute the next active pass from `_PLAN.json`.

`PLAN`: promote selected `TODO.json` or backlog work into explicit passes.

`BUG`: select and work the best active entry from `BUGS.json`.

`REVIEW`: inspect code, diff, plan, or architecture for risks first.

`AUDIT`: run `tools/documents/audit_docs.ps1`.

`FINISH`: run quality checks, audit changed memory, update state, and report residual risk.

`COMMIT`: create a bounded Git commit with explicit paths.

`LOG`: inspect supplied diagnostics and apply the smallest source-owned fix or diagnostic step.

`DIAGNOSTICS`: summarize newest diagnostic evidence, identify the highest-leverage issue, and choose the next source-owned fix or evidence step.

`PROMPT`: execute or defragment raw prompt intake from `_PROMPTS.md` and `_PROMPT-QUEUE.json`.

`INSTRUCTIONS`: defragment raw user instructions, sort them by dependency/future-change cost, promote them into the correct layer, and clear completed intake.

task_type_router.title: Task Type Router
task_type_router.body:
## Task Type Router

Use this router for programming projects in any language, framework, or runtime.

Keep routing lightweight: choose the task type, load the smallest useful context, then execute through the owning workflow. Do not duplicate framework-specific implementation doctrine here.

If a request does not fit any listed type, do not start broad reads or edits. State the mismatch, propose the best workflow with goal, sources, scope, completion criteria, verification, and likely memory updates, then ask the user to approve unless the task is small, local, reversible, and low-risk.

| Task type | Read first | Do |
|---|---|---|
| Implement feature | `STATE.json`, `_PLAN.json`, `WORKFLOW.json` when priority matters, smallest relevant `context-docs/`, then owning source files | Check priority and prerequisites, choose the owning boundary, prefer standard framework/library mechanisms when they fit, implement the smallest maintainable change, verify at the right gate |
| Analyze bug | `BUGS.json` or issue source, `STATE.json`, diagnostics if present, smallest relevant context/source slice | Separate observed behavior from hypothesis, reproduce or inspect evidence, fix root cause at the owning boundary, add targeted diagnostics if confidence is low |
| Analyze logs/crash/trace | `project/code/DIAGNOSTICS.json`, supplied logs/traces, `BUGS.json` if tracked | Extract current errors and timestamps, identify highest-leverage failing lifecycle, suggest or apply the smallest source-owned fix/evidence step |
| Analyze performance | `context-docs/PERFORMANCE.json` when present, relevant telemetry/profiling output, owning source slice | Report measured units, separate data from inference, look for hot paths, allocation churn, sync I/O, excessive polling, N+1 calls, cache misses, framework misuse, and heavyweight broad models being used where lightweight data/query/batch paths should own predictable load. Recommend migration before symptom shaving when the broad model is the bottleneck. |
| Audit | `DOCS.json`, `project/code/WORKFLOW.json`, audit scripts/configs | Run the relevant audit when available, fix local hard failures, report soft warnings as recommendations |
| Discuss vision/product direction | `WORKFLOW.json`, target/vision/product docs when present | Keep it conversational, separate facts from recommendations, do not edit plan or backlog without consent |
| Update documents | `DOCS.json`, target owning document, duplicate-candidate workflow when available | Choose one owner document, avoid duplicate memory, update generated caches only through tools |
| Update plan | `STATE.json`, `WORKFLOW.json`, `_PLAN.json`, `TODO.json` or backlog when needed | Preserve unresolved future scope, sequence by dependency and future-change cost, ask before material priority changes |
| Analyze plan | `STATE.json`, `WORKFLOW.json`, `_PLAN.json`, target/backlog docs when relevant | Identify missing prerequisites, stale passes, priority conflicts, risk concentration, and unclear verification |
| Analyze vision/architecture direction | target/vision docs, `WORKFLOW.json`, relevant architecture/decision records | Distinguish current target from long-horizon ideas, identify conflicts and tradeoffs, promote only after user approval |

general_workflow.title: General Workflow
general_workflow.body:
## General Workflow

1. Start from the smallest state source that answers the question.
2. Identify the ownership slice and task size: surgical fix, feature, refactor, migration, modernization, review, or diagnosis.
3. Load `core/PROJECT-PROFILE.json` when `project_profile` exists, and scale patterns to lifecycle, framework kind, and test reality.
4. Check existing scripts and project automation before doing repeatable work manually.
5. Before any code touch, load the full compiled code-guidance set when the project provides a knowledge layer; prefer `tools/knowledge/knowledge_code_context.py` and keep the returned rules active as implementation constraints, not optional background reading.
6. For non-obvious source-code writes, run a lightweight web research spike before custom design unless the active plan already names a research-backed mechanism. Ask concise business/product-direction and technical-implication questions first when the right architecture depends on future load, count, frequency, modules, dependencies, branches, or data volume.
7. Check borrowed/proven options before custom design. Treat local patterns and project philosophy as implementation filters after research, not automatic winners over established domain failure modes.
8. Run the better-version-now check per task: include small improvements to names, boundaries, contracts, data ownership, security, migrations, diagnostics, verification, or future change cost when cheap; ask before broad or speculative upgrades.
9. Respect profile gating: legacy surgical fixes stay surgical unless the user asked for refactor/modernization or a small seam directly reduces the fix risk.
10. Before implementation or performance diagnosis, apply lightweight futureproof. If behavior is likely to become batch, hot-path, storage-heavy, I/O-heavy, framework-heavy, count-heavy, dynamic, or repeatedly exercised, prefer the profile-appropriate bulk/query/index/cache/queue/helper/pooling/activation path. Ask for intended scale when architecture depends on it and scale is unknown.
11. For bug reports, first check whether the bug exposes a deeper ownership, lifecycle, contract, data-flow, tooling, observability, security, migration, performance-model, or architecture weakness; fix the class of bug only when that stays proportional to task size and profile.
12. For planning or feature triage, check `WORKFLOW.json` before changing active plan or backlog; challenge the request when it bypasses a higher-priority active pain point.
13. Load `team/*.json` role lenses only on demand or when workflow routing selects them.
14. Check whether a future-facing prerequisite should happen before the requested implementation.
15. For broad multi-step work, create or refresh `docs-tech/cache/current_task.json` and split the request into micro-tasks.
16. For structural or multi-boundary work, write a short impact note or plan before editing.
17. Rank recommendations or candidate passes by ROI when there are multiple options.
18. Implement within the scoped boundary, updating micro-task status when task work tracking is active.
19. Verify with the strongest practical gate at a natural verification boundary.
20. Run the artifact-based task-flow audit: cite loaded routing context, selected flow steps, closure evidence, changelog truth, tool-failure response, and tool-vs-manual discipline; for complex/high-risk tasks, run the changelog-based adversarial review.
21. Update only the memory layers whose current truth changed.
22. Finish with concrete changed files, verification, residual risk, and ROI Picks when follow-up recommendations exist.

script_first_execution.title: Script-First Execution
script_first_execution.body:
## Script-First Execution

For programming work, prefer project scripts whenever practical.

Use `tools/` helpers for pipeline tasks, package-manager scripts for ecosystem tasks, and framework CLIs for framework-native work.

Before running a script, verify its runtime is available when uncertain. If not, follow `core/SCRIPT-FALLBACK.json`: propose a system improvement with an install command and justification before falling back to manual work.

Do not manually duplicate a script's behavior unless the script is unavailable, unsafe for the current scope, broken, or explicitly rejected by the user.

Run scripts at natural workflow boundaries: before broad work when they provide routing context, during diagnostics when they summarize evidence, and near the end for check, audit, quality, finish, and commit steps.

For structured JSON documents, use `tools/documents/read_document_item.ps1` or `tools/documents/list_document_keys.ps1` before edits when selective reads are enough.

For structured JSON document search, use `tools/documents/review_document_list_candidates.ps1` or `tools/documents/check_document_list_item_duplicates.ps1` before text grep. `Select-String`, `grep`, and `rg` are fallback tools for JSON: use them only for raw formatting/encoding checks, parser or cache debugging, unknown references after document tools miss, or source-code searches.

For compiled context lookup, use `tools/knowledge/knowledge_select.py` before broad document reads when a named view or tag query can answer the task. Useful defaults are `--view session_start`, `--view debugging`, `--view performance`, `--view docs_workflow`, and project-specific views such as `architecture` or `domain_modeling` when matching entries exist.

Before answering a substantive question or executing a user command, use `tools/knowledge/context_tags.py <user text>` when available to derive context tags, then query `tools/knowledge/knowledge_select.py --tags <tags> --match any` for the relevant compiled notes. If the tagger is unavailable, create or repair it before falling back to manual tag selection when the current task is about pipeline context acquisition.

For programming tasks, always load the full compiled code-guidance set before code reads or edits with `tools/knowledge/knowledge_code_context.py`. If the wrapper is unavailable, use `tools/knowledge/knowledge_select.py --tags engineering,coding,architecture,best-practices,borrowed-before-invented,ddd-adiv,future-first,data-first,runtime,diagnostics,performance,verification --match any --type rule`. Treat the returned rules and best practices as active implementation constraints for code quality, ownership, architecture, readability, diagnostics, and verification.

Treat `AI-compiled/project/knowledge.sqlite` as a generated SQLite index. Source documents remain authoritative; refresh the index with `tools/knowledge/knowledge_compile.ps1` or targeted `tools/knowledge/knowledge_upsert.ps1 -Path <document>` after source changes.

If SQLite is unavailable or locked, use the file fallback under `AI-compiled/project/knowledge-files/` with `tools/knowledge/knowledge_file_select.ps1`, `tools/knowledge/knowledge_file_check.ps1`, and related `knowledge_file_*` scripts. The fallback uses the same table-shaped data model but does not support named views.

For editing structured JSON documents, use the document item tools instead of ad hoc inline PowerShell for structural item edits. Treat them as transaction wrappers that edit, normalize as UTF-8, rebuild cache when applicable, run compact local checks, and print a final result.

For adding multiple keyed items to one structured JSON document, use `tools/documents/bulk_insert_document_items.ps1`.

For moving or copying items between structured JSON documents, use `tools/documents/migrate_document_item.ps1`.

For moving items inside one structured JSON document, use `tools/documents/move_document_item.ps1`.

For inserting items into structured JSON documents, use `tools/documents/insert_document_item.ps1`.
- For simple reference, inspiration, source, URL, or one-line list additions inside an existing structured JSON item, use `tools/documents/insert_checked_document_list_item.ps1` when available to merge insert and compact task verification; otherwise use `tools/documents/insert_document_list_item.ps1`; use explicit duplicate review first only when possible matches need manual selection.
- For documentation updates outside context capsules and strictly technical agent memory, use the candidate-title flow: derive duplicate terms, run `tools/documents/review_document_list_candidates.ps1 -Terms <words>` when available so duplicate candidates and matching sections are reviewed in one command; otherwise run `tools/documents/check_document_list_item_duplicates.ps1 -Terms <words>` and read candidates with `tools/documents/read_document_items_by_title.ps1 -Titles <titles>`, then write once with a transactional insert/item script.
- Hard rule: documentation reads/writes outside context capsules and strictly technical agent memory must use the candidate-title workflow; prefer `tools/documents/review_document_list_candidates.ps1` over separate check/read-title commands when the wrapper exists.

verification_boundary.title: Verification Boundary
verification_boundary.body:
## Verification Boundary

Treat status, audit, quality, line-index, task-flow audit, and finish scripts as boundary tools, not as reflex tools after every small edit.

Default cadence:

1. Use targeted reads, outlines, search, and diffs while exploring and editing.
2. Make all code and document edits for the current scope before running broad checks.
3. Run `tools/repo/task_flow_audit.ps1` or answer its checklist at closure: loaded routing context, selected flow steps, closure evidence, changelog truth, tool-failure response, and tool-vs-manual discipline. Use `-Complex` or the heuristic complex section for architecture-relevant, hot-path, performance-sensitive, broad, migration, or future-foundation diffs. When closure needs a machine-readable gate, run `task_flow_audit.ps1 -RequireClosureEvidence -ClosureEvidencePath <json>` or pass `-RequireTaskFlowEvidence -TaskFlowEvidencePath <json>` through the finalizer.
4. Run `tools/repo/finalize_task_check_commit_push.ps1 -Message "<message>"` near the end of the scope when a Git-backed task should be committed and pushed. It should discover changed files from git, normalize text, rebuild document cache, refresh code-line indexes, run audit/quality, task-flow audit, stage intentional files, commit, push, and report status.
5. For broad multi-boundary tasks, run one finalizer after each coherent subtask block before moving to the next block.
6. Rerun a gate only after fixing something that could plausibly affect that gate.

Avoid full-file reads immediately after a successful structured read unless the structured output is missing the needed section, the document shape is suspicious, or exact raw formatting matters.

Do not manually repeat normalize/cache/index/check steps after each micro-edit or after a successful transactional document edit tool unless the wrapper failed, `-NoPostEdit` was intentionally used, or a broader verification boundary requires it.

If `tools/repo/finalize_task_check_commit_push.ps1` or an owned finalizer fails on a repeated mechanical issue, update the script so the same class of issue is handled automatically before rerunning the finalizer.

task_work_tracking.title: Task Work Tracking
task_work_tracking.body:
## Task Work Tracking

For broad tasks, use `core/TASK-WORK.json`.

Default temporary file:

```text
docs-tech/cache/current_task.json
```

Use `tools/pipeline/init_task_work.ps1` when practical.

For broad fragmented work that crosses the grouped-fragmentation trigger, use `tools/tasks/grouped_task_handoff.ps1` after each bounded sub-task to update `docs-tech/cache/grouped_task_handoff.json` and `docs-tech/cache/next_subtask_prompt.md`. User approval is required before each group, but sub-tasks inside the approved group may continue automatically through the handoff until an escalation condition appears.

The agent should:

1. Create a micro-task plan before broad implementation.
2. Track one active micro-task at a time.
3. Update statuses and short results during work.
4. Track questions, risks, and verification gaps.
5. Before final response, resolve every micro-task as done, skipped, blocked, or deferred.
6. Remove the temporary file when it has no future value, or promote only durable facts to the proper memory layer.

branch_scoped_workflow.title: Branch-Scoped Workflow
branch_scoped_workflow.body:
## Branch-Scoped Workflow

When `workflow.branch_mode` is `branch_scoped`:

1. Detect the current Git branch at session start.
2. Look for `ignored/ai-socratex/<branch>-STATE.md` and `ignored/ai-socratex/<branch>-PLAN.md`, or the configured equivalents.
3. Read them when they exist.
4. Create them from `templates/code/branch/` when missing.
5. Update branch STATE and PLAN continuously, not only at the end.
6. Keep STATE factual: findings, root causes, changes, verification, blockers.
7. Keep PLAN as next steps only, with no history.
8. At branch end, optionally promote durable findings into `context-docs/`, `DECISIONS.json`, `CHANGELOG.json` when `changelog.enabled` is not `no`, `_PLAN.json`, or `TODO.json`.

If the branch files language is not English, ensure the branch files directory is ignored by Git before writing local notes.

roi_workflow.title: ROI Workflow
roi_workflow.body:
## ROI Workflow

`REVIEW` must end with ROI Picks when it contains findings or recommendations.

`PLAN` must assign ROI metadata before promoting backlog items into `_PLAN.json`.

Promotion should prefer high-ROI work for the current `project_profile`, especially work that improves the user's stated highest pain.

context_cost_control.title: Context Cost Control
context_cost_control.body:
## Context Cost Control

Use `core/CONTEXT-COMPACTION.json` when a session becomes long, after several major passes, or after repeated retry loops.

Prefer a hard reset after compacting `STATE.json` and relevant `context-docs/` over carrying stale live context indefinitely.
